Malaysia,7,7.1,Personal Data Protection Act 2010,Act 709,,Cross-cutting,Section 5(1),KNOWN,p. 17,"5. (1) The processing of personal data by a data user shall be in compliance with the following Personal Data Protection Principles, namely—
(a) the General Principle
(b) the Notice and Choice Principle
(c) the Disclosure Principle
(d) the Security Principle
(e) the Retention Principle
(f) the Data Integrity Principle
(g) the Access Principle,

as set out in sections 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11 and 12.","This provision establishes the core comprehensive data protection framework by mandating compliance with seven fundamental principles governing the processing of personal data, including consent, notice, disclosure, security, retention, integrity, and access. It serves as the foundational legal basis for data subject rights and data user obligations across all commercial transactions in Malaysia.","Section 5(1): 5. (1) The processing of personal data by a data user shall be in compliance with the following Personal Data Protection Principles, namely—
(a) the General Principle
(b) the Notice and Choice Principle
(c) the Disclosure Principle
(d) the Security Principle
(e) the Retention Principle
(f) the Data Integrity Principle
(g) the Access Principle,
as set out in sections 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11 and 12.

Interpretation: This provision establishes the core comprehensive data protection framework by mandating compliance with seven fundamental principles governing the processing of personal data, including consent, notice, disclosure, security, retention, integrity, and access. It serves as the foundational legal basis for data subject rights and data user obligations across all commercial transactions in Malaysia.",Since June 2010

Malaysia,7,7.2,Computer Crimes Act 1997,Act 563,,Cross-cutting,Section 3(1),KNOWN,p. 9,3. (1) A person shall be guilty of an offence if— (a) he causes a computer to perform any function with intent to secure access to any program or data held in any computer
column2: (b) the access he intends to secure is unauthorized
column3: and (c) he knows at the time when he causes the computer to perform the function that is the case.,"This provision establishes a cybersecurity framework by defining and penalizing the computer misuse offence of unauthorized access to computer material. By criminalizing illicit intrusions, it provides a foundational legal deterrent against cyberattacks and protects the integrity of computer systems and data.","Section 3(1): 3. (1) A person shall be guilty of an offence if— (a) he causes a computer to perform any function with intent to secure access to any program or data held in any computer
column4: (b) the access he intends to secure is unauthorized
column5: and (c) he knows at the time when he causes the computer to perform the function that is the case.

column1: Interpretation: This provision establishes a cybersecurity framework by defining and penalizing the computer misuse offence of unauthorized access to computer material. By criminalizing illicit intrusions, it provides a foundational legal deterrent against cyberattacks and protects the integrity of computer systems and data.",Since 1 June 2000

Malaysia,7,7.2,Cyber Security Act 2024,Act 854,,Cross-cutting,Long Title,KNOWN,p. 7,"An Act to enhance the national cyber security by providing for the establishment of the National Cyber Security Committee, duties and powers of the Chief Executive of the National Cyber Security Agency, functions and duties of the national critical information infrastructure sector leads and national critical information infrastructure entities and the management of cyber security threats and cyber security incidents to national critical information infrastructures, to regulate the cyber security service providers through licensing, and to provide for related matters.","The Cyber Security Act 2024 establishes a comprehensive national cybersecurity framework by mandating the protection of National Critical Information Infrastructure (NCII), requiring the implementation of security codes of practice, incident reporting, and the licensing of cybersecurity service providers. This demonstrates a dedicated, cross-sectoral regulatory regime aimed at enhancing national cybersecurity and managing cyber threats.","Long Title: An Act to enhance the national cyber security by providing for the establishment of the National Cyber Security Committee, duties and powers of the Chief Executive of the National Cyber Security Agency, functions and duties of the national critical information infrastructure sector leads and national critical information infrastructure entities and the management of cyber security threats and cyber security incidents to national critical information infrastructures, to regulate the cyber security service providers through licensing, and to provide for related matters.

Interpretation: The Cyber Security Act 2024 establishes a comprehensive national cybersecurity framework by mandating the protection of National Critical Information Infrastructure (NCII), requiring the implementation of security codes of practice, incident reporting, and the licensing of cybersecurity service providers. This demonstrates a dedicated, cross-sectoral regulatory regime aimed at enhancing national cybersecurity and managing cyber threats.",Not specified in text,https://lokekinggoh.com/layout2/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/Act-854.pdf,,PASS,"An Act to enhance the national cyber security by providing for the establishment of the National Cyber Security Committee, duties and powers of the Chief Executive of the National Cyber Security Agency, functions and duties of the national critical information infrastructure sector leads and national critical information infrastructure entities and the management of cyber security threats and cyber security incidents to national critical information infrastructures, to regulate the cyber security service providers through licensing, and to provide for related matters.","The claimed snippet exactly matches the Long Title of the Cyber Security Act 2024 found on page 7 of the document, with only minor whitespace and line break differences."

Malaysia,7,7.3,Personal Data Protection Standard 2015,JPDP.100-1/1/10 (2),,Cross-cutting,Section 6.1(6),KNOWN,p. 8,6. Prepare a personal data disposal schedule for inactive data with a 24 month period. The personal data disposal schedule should be maintained properly.,"This provision requires data users to establish a disposal schedule for inactive personal data with a specified 24-month period, effectively setting a minimum retention period of 24 months for inactive data prior to disposal. Although the overarching standard emphasizes storage limitation and maximum retention periods, this specific clause mandates a minimum timeframe for retaining inactive data.","Section 6.1(6): 6. Prepare a personal data disposal schedule for inactive data with a 24 month period. The personal data disposal schedule should be maintained properly.

Interpretation: This provision requires data users to establish a disposal schedule for inactive personal data with a specified 24-month period, effectively setting a minimum retention period of 24 months for inactive data prior to disposal. Although the overarching standard emphasizes storage limitation and maximum retention periods, this specific clause mandates a minimum timeframe for retaining inactive data.",Since December 2015
column2: Last amended Not specified in text,https://www.viatris.com/-/media/project/common/viatris/pdf/malaysia/malaysian-personal-data-protection-act-updated-2015.pdf,,PASS,6. Prepare a personal data disposal schedule for inactive data with a 24 month period. The personal data disposal schedule should be maintained properly.,"The claimed snippet exactly matches the text found under Section 6.1, item 6 of the Retention Standard in the document, with only a minor line-break difference."

column1: Malaysia,7,7.4,Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024,Act A1727,2024,Cross-cutting,Section 12a,KNOWN,p. 6,"12a. (1) A data controller shall appoint one or more data protection officers who shall be accountable to the data controller for the compliance with this Act.

(2) Where the processing of personal data is carried out by a data processor on behalf of the data controller, the data processor shall appoint one or more data protection officers who shall be accountable to the data processor for the compliance with this Act.","This provision establishes a mandatory requirement for both data controllers and data processors to appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO) to ensure accountability and compliance with the Act. While it does not explicitly mandate Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs), the DPO requirement serves as a core accountability mechanism for data protection governance.","Section 12a: 12a. (1) A data controller shall appoint one or more data protection officers who shall be accountable to the data controller for the compliance with this Act.

(2) Where the processing of personal data is carried out by a data processor on behalf of the data controller, the data processor shall appoint one or more data protection officers who shall be accountable to the data processor for the compliance with this Act.

Interpretation: This provision establishes a mandatory requirement for both data controllers and data processors to appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO) to ensure accountability and compliance with the Act. While it does not explicitly mandate Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs), the DPO requirement serves as a core accountability mechanism for data protection governance.",Not specified in text,https://www.pdp.gov.my/ppdpv1/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/Act-A1727.pdf,,PASS,"12a. (1) A data controller shall appoint one or more
data protection officers who shall be accountable to the
data controller for the compliance with this Act.
(2) Where the processing of personal data is carried out
by a data processor on behalf of the data controller, the data
processor shall appoint one or more data protection officers who
shall be accountable to the data processor for the compliance
with this Act.","The claimed snippet matches the actual text of Section 12a(1) and (2) found in the document verbatim, with only minor whitespace and line break differences."

Malaysia,7,7.5,Security Offences (Special Measures) Act 2012,Act 747,,Cross-cutting,Section 6(2),KNOWN,p. 10,"6. (2) The public prosecutor, if he considers that it is likely to contain any information relating to the communication of a security offence, may— (a) require a communications service provider to intercept and retain a specified communication or communications of a specified description received or transmitted, or about to be received or transmitted by that communications service provider
column2: or (b) authorize a police officer to enter any premises and to install on such premises, any device for the interception and retention of a specified communication or communications of a specified description and to remove and retain such evidence.","This provision grants the public prosecutor the power to compel communications service providers to intercept and retain specified communications, or to authorize police to install interception devices on premises. This establishes a lawful interception and surveillance regime that permits government authorities to access personal data held by private organizations for the investigation of security offences.","Section 6(2): 6. (2) The public prosecutor, if he considers that it is likely to contain any information relating to the communication of a security offence, may— (a) require a communications service provider to intercept and retain a specified communication or communications of a specified description received or transmitted, or about to be received or transmitted by that communications service provider
column3: or (b) authorize a police officer to enter any premises and to install on such premises, any device for the interception and retention of a specified communication or communications of a specified description and to remove and retain such evidence.

column1: Malaysia,7,7.5,Security Offences (Special Measures) Act 2012,Act 747,2015,Cross-cutting,Section 6,KNOWN,p. 12-13,"6. (1) Notwithstanding any other written law, the Public Prosecutor, if he considers that it is likely to contain any information relating to the commission of a security offence, may authorize any police officer or any other person— (a) to intercept, detain and open any postal article in the course of transmission by post
column2: (b) to intercept any message transmitted or received by any communication
column3: or (c) to intercept or listen to any conversation by any communication. (2) The Public Prosecutor, if he considers that it is likely to contain any information relating to the communication of a security offence, may— (a) require a communications service provider to intercept and retain a specified communication or communications of a specified description received or transmitted, or about to be received or transmitted by that communications service provider
column4: or (b) authorize a police officer to enter any premises and to install on such premises, any device for the interception and retention of a specified communication or communications of a specified description and to remove and retain such evidence.",This provision grants the Public Prosecutor the authority to authorize police officers to intercept communications and require communications service providers to intercept and retain specified communications. This constitutes a lawful interception and surveillance power that allows government access to personal data held by private organizations for the investigation of security offences.,"Section 6: 6. column6: or (c) to intercept or listen to any conversation by any communication. column7: or (b) authorize a police officer to enter any premises and to install on such premises, any device for the interception and retention of a specified communication or communications of a specified description and to remove and retain such evidence.
